Party_Details.Name: Lim Kok Leong

Facts.Text: From 1990 to 1993, Lim Kok Leong worked as a salesperson for the partnership business that later became Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
In 1993, Lim Kok Leong joined HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd as managing director.
In 1996, Lim Kok Leong received 10,000 shares in Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd from the fourth defendant without payment, in recognition of his work for the prior partnership business.
In 1996, Lim Kok Leong was appointed as a director of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not participate in most directors' meetings of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not attend the annual general meetings of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not request access to the accounting or other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd until July 2014.
From about 1997 onwards, Lim Kok Leong did not undertake financial liabilities on behalf of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and did not sign official documents on its behalf.
On 16 January 2003, Lim Kok Leong became a director of Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd.
On 3 August 2004, Lim Kok Leong ceased to be a director of Acmetec Corporation Pte Ltd.
From 1989 to 1991, Lim Kok Leong operated Tricess Enterprise as a sole proprietorship.
On 1 July 2014, Lim Kok Leong requested inspection of the accounting and other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd for the last five years.
After 1 July 2014, further similar requests by Lim Kok Leong to inspect the accounting and other records of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd were not granted.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a passive director of a company is entitled to inspect the company’s accounting and financial records, and whether that right can be enforced against both the company and its active directors personally.
Party_Details.Rule: A company and its directors must keep accounting and other records that explain the company’s transactions and financial position, and those records must remain open to inspection by any director at all times. A director has a nearly absolute right to inspect these records to enable the performance of their fiduciary duties. This right can only be denied if the opposing party provides clear proof that the inspection is sought for an improper purpose intended to injure the company.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Kok Leong was appointed a director in 1996 but acted as a 'sleeping' director with virtually no involvement in management, meetings, or financial liabilities for over 15 years. In July 2014, he suddenly requested access to the company’s accounting records for the preceding five years. The company and its active directors refused the request, citing his historical lack of involvement, his recent removal from an affiliated company, and his past involvement in other businesses, inferring an ulterior and detrimental motive for the sudden request.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that a director’s right to inspect company records is a fundamental and mandatory incident of directorship, necessary to enable the discharge of fiduciary duties. The court rejected the argument that a 'sleeping' director, or one who historically lacked interest, loses this right. The right is virtually absolute and can only be denied if the opposing parties provide clear proof that the inspection is sought for an improper purpose that would substantially harm the company. The court found the active directors failed to meet this burden; the allegations of ulterior motives based on past business affiliations were unmeritorious. Furthermore, the statutory duty to keep and provide records applies to the active directors personally, not just the corporate entity. Therefore, the court granted the application, compelling both the company and its active directors to provide access to the records.

Party_Details.Name: Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and others

Facts.Text: Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1996 as a company engaged in general wholesale business, including trading in electronic components, electrical cables, network cables, and accessories.
Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd operated as a family-run business.
Before the incorporation of Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd in 1996, the business operated as a partnership founded by the fourth defendant and the third defendant, who are husband and wife.
During the partnership period before 1996, the fourth defendant led and managed the partnership business that later became Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd.
HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd was incorporated in 1993, and the second, third, and fifth defendants were shareholders of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd.
Around 1993, the second, third, and fifth defendants, as shareholders of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd, were involved in appointing Lim Kok Leong as managing director of HLC Enterprises Pte Ltd.
On 1 July 2014, Seen Joo Company Pte Ltd and its active directors did not grant Lim Kok Leong’s request to inspect the accounting and other records of the company for the last five years.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a company and its active management can lawfully refuse a co-director's request to inspect corporate financial records on the grounds of his prolonged non-participation and alleged ulterior motives.
Party_Details.Rule: A company and its active directors are required to keep accounting records that are open to inspection by all directors at all times. The right of a director to inspect is nearly absolute and can only be restricted if the active management proves with clear evidence that the inspection is sought for an improper purpose that would be detrimental to the company.
Party_Details.Application: The company was a family-run business where the active directors managed daily operations, while the requesting director had remained entirely passive since 1997. When the passive director suddenly requested five years of accounting records in 2014, the active directors denied the request. They asserted that his prolonged disinterest, combined with his recent removal from an affiliated company and his history with other entities, indicated that the inspection was not for the proper discharge of director duties but for an improper, detrimental purpose. They also asserted that any obligation to provide records belonged solely to the corporate entity, preventing the active directors from being joined personally.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court determined that the obligation to maintain and allow inspection of corporate records extends explicitly to the company's active directors and managers personally, allowing them to be compelled directly. The court affirmed that a director's right to inspect is not diminished by a history of non-participation or 'sleeping' status. To lawfully refuse inspection, the active directors bore the burden of proving with clear evidence that the request was made for an improper purpose intended to injure the company. The court concluded that the active directors' suspicions based on the requesting director's other business dealings did not satisfy this high evidentiary threshold. The active directors and the company were ordered to produce the requested records.
